frameworks.name: Bayesian posterior probability of correctness
frameworks.one_line: Optimal observer computes full posterior to decide when to stop sampling

frameworks.name: evidence accumulation with collapsing threshold
frameworks.one_line: Integration to a time-varying bound approximates the Bayesian stopping rule

frameworks.name: adaptive gain by inverse prediction error
frameworks.one_line: Sensory samples are weighted by reliability inferred from prediction error

frameworks.name: optional stopping under heteroscedasticity
frameworks.one_line: Choice timing in environments with time-varying signal reliability

core_question: How do humans decide when to stop accumulating perceptual evidence when sample reliability varies unpredictably over time?
key_assumptions: evidence reliability can change within a trial (heteroscedastic input)
observer trades information harvest against deliberation cost
stopping policy can be approximated by a collapsing decision threshold
manipulations: categorisation from a serial stream of oriented grating samples
free choice of stopping time on each trial
key_findings: best-fitting model weighted samples by inverse prediction error and integrated to a collapsing bound
this model approximated the full Bayesian posterior of being correct
the same adaptive weighting predicted decision-related neural signals
interpretations: adaptive reliability weighting may have evolved to support optional stopping in heteroscedastic environments
human optional stopping is well-described as a tractable approximation to Bayesian-optimal stopping
